content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Looking for a weird movie to watch tonight!

**Score:** 73
**Body:** I’m dog-sitting for my parents this weekend, staying by myself, and looking to watch something weird and crazy that my wife probably wouldn’t care for. I’m open to any genre, country, or time period, as well as just outright awful movies. I’d much rather a film be bad than boring. Give me a fascinating train wreck over a slice of dry toast any day.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fanastic Planet

Eraserhead

Swiss Army Man

The Lobster

  - especially Eraserhead, one of the strangest films I've ever seen
  - Came here to suggest The Lobster as it is both weird and entertaining (a lot of movies think it is enough to just be weird for the sake lf weird)

I havent seen the other ones except for Fantastic Planet, which I would put squareley in the weird for the sake of weird category

Edit to add: Being John Malkovich is also both weird and entertaining
2. Bubba Ho-Tep is a bit weird and somewhat out of the ordinary.
3. Eating Raoul
4. House (J-horror from 1977). 
5. The Holy Mountain

https://boxd.it/20mu
6. Dave Made a Maze

Swiss Army Man

Everything, Everywhere, All at Once

Psycho Goreman
  - Dave Made a Maze was so off beat and brilliant.
7. Gummo

## 2. A simple movie with no plots. It’s a feel good type of movie

**Score:** 35
**Body:** It doesn’t need to have twist and turns or any plot structure. I am not looking for action or drama or thrillers. It exists to make you feel good. It should be peaceful to watch and relaxes your mind.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dazed and Confused 
2. Amelie is my go-to for this type of movie! 
3. *Perfect Days*
4. Waking Ned Devine 
5. I Love you Man
6. The Terminal
7. Local Hero
8. The way way back. 
9. Paterson
10. Chef. Aside from the opening bit there’s literally no conflict 

## 3. Movies that are critical of the police

**Score:** 25
**Body:** I’m looking for any and all films that are all or partially critical of the police. Or do an untraditional cop film. 

If you are unsure if it fits, write it anyway! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Copland (1997)

Serpico (1973)

L.A. Confidential (1997)
  - Serpico is a great one to start with 
2. Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri is the one that immediately comes to mind. 
3. Do the Right Thing
4. Training day 
5. [removed]
6. Bad Lieutenant 

7. Hot Fuzz?
8. First Blood
9. The Blues Brothers

## 4. Looking for wholesome, heartfelt movies

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Looking for a good heart-warming, yet adult film. Adult as in a healthy and tasteful use of adult themes, profanity, etc., not xxx. I just recently watched Nonna's and I loved it, and also absolutely loved Stranger than Fiction. Fundamentals of Caring was a really good one. For added context, I've really enjoyed TV shows like Grace and Frankie and The Four Seasons (season 2 doesn't come out until next week). 
EDIT: looking for primarily comedies.

TIA!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hidden Figures
2. Remarkably Bright Creatures just dropped on Netflix,  it is amazingly adorable.
3. Little Miss Sunshine (2006)
Parenthood (1989)
4. Awakenings  w/Robin Williams & Robert De Niro 👍
5. Hunt for the Wilderpeople
6. The Darjeeling Limited
7. St. Vincent with Bill Murray and Melissa McCarthy. Excellent film, funny and moving.
  - Ted Lasso. I know it’s not a movie but is the most heartfelt of heartfelt shows. 
8. Project Hail Mary 
9. Thelma (2024)

## 5. Looking for Arthouse Mind-Bending Movies That Completely Break Your Brain

**Score:** 14
**Body:** I’m looking for films that feel both arthouse and genuinely mind-bending — not just “plot twist” movies, but films that mess with perception, identity, time, reality, memory, dreams, paranoia, existentialism, etc.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The film Naked Lunch is super good. It’s a combo of the book and a bit of bio on Burroughs and it has talking alien insect typewriters, Ian Holm and so much more. It fits almost exactly what you’re describing 
2. Maybe more mainstream, but David Lynch’s Mulholland Drive is really good
3. primer
4. 'The Holy Mountain' (1973)
5. Stalker by Tarkovsky. Many other of his films would fit also
6. Persona, by Bergman, Three Women, Robert Altman.
7. Upstream Color 
8. I am thinking of ending things
9. Beau Is Afraid

Southland Tales

Eraserhead


10. A lot of David Lynch has you covered here. Mulholland Drive, Inland Empire, Season 3 of Twin Peaks...

And yeah, that last one is technically television but he basically said they made an 18 hour long movie and just arbitrarily sliced it into episode chunks.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Watched 12 Years a Slave for the first time, and I must say Lupita Nyong’o is truly a powerhouse performer. Fassbender and Chiwetel both were great too.

**Score:** 741
**Body:** One of the best biographical dramas I’ve ever watched.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Patsey, the woman portrayed by Lupita Nyong'o, lived long enough to see freedom. In 1863, during the siege of Port Hudson, Captain Henry C Devendorf of the 110th NY Infantry, wrote a letter detailing her fate of being sent north just days before. 


2. I don't know how I ended up watching this, then Fruitvale Station an hour after..pretty shitty choice for a double feature and don't recommend to combo to anyone. Too painful for one day..
3. ![gif](giphy|f4JrFIyHwgbig7TUFU)  
Lupita can also be super funny 
4. Some performances are so raw they stop feeling like performances at all, and Lupita absolutely did that here
5. Fassbender is terrifying here. The scene where he chases Chiwetel across the farm is pure anxiety fuel.
6. Hollywood has no idea how to use her. Lupita Nyong’o would KILL in romcoms. But because she’s dark skinned they can’t see that she should be getting the Meg Ryan roles. 
  - She’s quietly become one of the top “scream queens” of her generation ! Her horror movies are all excellent !
7. This is a top 5 amazing movie that I don’t want to watch again. 
8. Wasn't this her first role? Or at least major role?
9. The best supporting actress performance of the decade, bar none. 

## 2. Is Dr. Sleep Mike Flanagan’s best thing ever?

**Score:** 511
**Body:** Almost everything about this movie is 10/10. Hits you hard emotionally right at the start, strikes again in the middle, it has fantastic bad guys, and just the best visual language. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I thought Midnight Mass was shockingly original.
  - Just finished it last night. I think I liked it more than Hill House, which I loved 
  - I appreciate a show that is just a bunch of monologues about the existential nature of our fragile lives.
2. For me at least, it’s his best film. Best thing? That would be a toss up between Hill House and Midnight Mass, IMO.
3. I heard directors cut is best to watch? Only saw theatrical and was kinda indifferent 
  - I only watched the Director’s Cut, but it was incredible. 
    - Director’s cut adds all the little details that make the overall story that much more enjoyable. My wife and I loved it. 10/10 movie. Honestly one of my favorite psychological horror movies
  - It’s the superior version, it’s formatted like a book. 
  - So much of why it think there is a split is the 30-40 extra mins many saw that the theatrical release people didn’t. Directors cut is night and day better than theatrical, like Kingdom of heaven or Alien 3 levels different 
4. I love this film but still not his best work. I think Haunting of Hill House is still his best work. The Two Storms episode is a masterclass in filmmaking. 

## 3. What is the best action in a movie you’ve ever seen?

**Score:** 384
**Body:** I was lucky enough to see the upcoming movie, The Furious at TIFF last year and I said it was the best action I’ve ever seen. A bunch of people asked how it compares to The Raid so I just watched it and it definitely has some great action, but The Furious is better imo. 

There’s just more of it and there’s so many creative ways they use props and weapons. So if you liked The Raid then definitely check out The Furious when it comes out in June. What’s the movie with best action you’ve ever seen?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Terminator 2

*Processing img f9ktp6vfnw2h1...*
2. Both Raid movies and Drunken Master II are the top hand-to-hand combat movies for me.

Gunwise: John Wick series and Hard Boiled.

Car chase: Matrix Reloaded.   There's a lot going on in this 10 minute plus sequence.  You have three groups driving, fighting, and shooting,
3. [Aliens](https://youtu.be/oSeQQlaCZgU?si=oFHlWRGsEhXXnT7w) (1986)

At this point, it’s become a tradition that any time I get a bigger screen TV, this is the first thing I watch on it.
4. The Legend of Drunken Master

https://i.redd.it/06j2oan9nw2h1.gif
5. The Matrix

![gif](giphy|V2ojLo7PvhVug)
6. Die Hard
7. Ong Baak for anyone that loves muay Thai! 
8. Dredd
9. Police Story. In the first ten minutes alone you have the cars through the shanty town and the bus chase, with Jackie Chan hanging off the side by an umbrella.


Then you have one of the best fights on film, where Chan takes on the crimimal gang in a shopping mall. Every aspiring action filmmaker should watch it, the camera movement and razor sharp editing are absolutely perfect.


https://youtu.be/B215g-Evv0U?si=7SN3EkH0__2Iaj8L
10. There is only one answer,  Fury Road.

## 4. What’s your favorite calm before the storm scene?

**Score:** 336
**Body:** What’s your favorite scene where all seems quiet and normal until everything goes south completely?

For me it’s the motel sequence in Drive (2011). The slow burn and lack of soundtrack kinda puts you in a trance and then like a jump scare, a bloody shootout happens.

What’s that scene for you?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/ae9ien8jsy2h1.png?width=1411&format=png&auto=webp&s=629362299165e7e5e05a2dbac49ede86137efc91


  - “Hello Carson…

Let’s go to your room.”

This scene gives me chills.
    - “Is Carson Wells there?” 

*Looks at his corpse*

“Not in the sense that you mean.” 
2. ![gif](giphy|fzp61UL3T4Wlcw9SvX)
3. The Jaws scene where the three are eating, drinking, etc. on the Orca the night before everything goes down if that counts.
4. ![gif](giphy|IHiGS6HMtXeBq)


5. https://preview.redd.it/0f48dwx2wy2h1.png?width=3840&format=png&auto=webp&s=ee3b68a0d0290c20100f67998242044aae023497

Right before killing Bonnie and Clyde in The Highwaymen.
6. Wind River.

"Why you flanking me?"
7. The Border Crossing Scene in Sicario
8. Does the shootout in heat count ? Just some guys walking out of a bank  with some bags and then all hell breaks loose 

## 5. In honor of memorial day..what is your favorite movie to watch this weekend?

**Score:** 230
**Body:** Mine is Glory...that ending with the cannon firing....geez! 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Band of Brothers (I know it's a miniseries, but still) 
  - Spike or whatever it was called before that, used to rerun it every military based holiday and I’d always watch. Currahee!!!
2. Well I coincidentally watched Attack of the Clones and that’s probably the last time I ever need to watch it
3. Glory - one of the top war movies ever made.
4. Read the history of the 54th.  It gets better.
  - Do you have a book recommendation?
5. I'm gonna watch The Big Lebowski
6. I watch the same thing every year, Monaco GP/Indy 500/Coke 600 with my wife in the other room.
7. Fellowship of the Ring 
8. Starship Troopers

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. To Live and Die in L.A. and The Departed

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I just watched To Live and Die in L.A. and thought it was excellent. While the overall premise of the movie is different, I couldn’t help but notice the ending draws a lot of parallels to The Departed.

>!Both films include sudden and shocking deaths of the protagonist followed by a side character avenging their death by killing the antagonist.!<

Do you think The Departed’s ending was directly influenced by this film? Also if you have any other recommendations for similar films from the 70s - 90s I’d love to hear them.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Probably not.  The Departed is a remake of a Hong Kong film called Infernal Affairs (2002).  The original film's ending is mostly the same, except the Marky Mark character didn't exist, so the Matt Damon character >!got away with everything, but he was stuck in a fragile lie that he knew could collapse on him at any second.!<

I'm not going to rule out Infernal Affairs being influenced by To Live and Die in LA in some way, but it's nothing obvious if it's there.
  - Serpico did it as well although I have no idea if it influenced either one.
    - Umm... how?
2. for that mix of undercover chaos and bleak, nobody-wins energy you get in both, try The Friends of Eddie Coyle (1973, Yates) for its unglamorous grinding betrayal and half-botched crime jobs, or Killing them Softly (2012, Dominik) which updates those American criminal betrayals into modern nihilism but really vibes with that doomed cop/crook dance, and from the 80s heat Michael Mann’s Thief (1981) goes all in on the existential endgame of a professional criminal, with that same sense of everyone being ground up by their own code. also check out Charley Varrick (1973, Don Siegel)—sharp little crime thriller with weirdly similar rug-pull violence.
  - Thief and To Live and Die in LA are two of my favorite films. They both have great soundtracks too which blend well with the films.
3. Yes and no because “infernal affairs” on which departed was directly adapted from has the exact same ending HOWEVER that Hong Kong predecessor was also made before to live and die in LA so I wouldn’t be shocked if Infernal Affairs took that from TLADILA. That’s an interesting connection you made and yeah just totally shocking by the actual
Camera work too. No dramatic close ups or anything. I think both were shot wide and then it just happens. I rewound and tried to see if I saw that correctly! 
4. I never noticed that parallel until you just pointed it out. That's pretty cool. I remember being SO SHOCKED by both deaths when I watched each movie for the first time. I was like "Wait, but.....there's more movie left. That doesn't....that can't....." wonderfully disorienting.

## 2. From A Touch of Sin to Walking Past the Future: The Fate and Love of Poor Rural Young Men and Women from China Drifting Through Cities

**Score:** 11
**Body:** In May 2026, I happened to watch  Walking Past the Future(路过未来), a film released in 2017. The main storyline follows a young man and woman who met online and fell in love offline. Both came from rural areas of mainland China and worked in Shenzhen to earn a living, experiencing many hardships and twists of fate. Watching this film immediately reminded me of another movie, A Touch of Sin (天注定), which also contains a subplot about a young couple in love working in Shenzhen.

The stories of working youth and romance in these two films contain both similarities and differences. In A Touch of Sin, the young man Xiaohui (小辉) is a rather naïve and honest Foxconn worker, while the young woman Lianrong (莲蓉) is a sex worker serving powerful men. The film has a darker tone and more oppressive atmosphere, ending with the tragedy of the young man’s suicide. In Walking Past the Future, the young man Xinmin (新民) and the young woman Yaoting (耀婷) also struggle to survive, but they are more lively and optimistic. The film alternates between gloom and hope, and despite enduring many hardships, the lovers remain devoted to each other and move toward marriage.

However, both films coincidentally reflect the same reality: many young people from ordinary rural families, lacking connections and resources, find themselves alone in big cities, struggling to survive and uncertain about the future.

For most young migrant workers entering cities, the main path available is factory labor, exchanging exhausting work on assembly lines for meager sweatshop wages. Such work is somewhat better than laboring in the fields “with faces toward the yellow earth and backs toward the sky” in rural areas, and the income is somewhat higher. This was precisely why their parents and the older generation of migrant workers eagerly entered cities for work. But younger generations find it harder to tolerate such repetitive and exhausting labor and instead hope for easier work and quicker money. This is why Xiaohui and Lianrong in A Touch of Sin, as well as Xinmin and Yaoting in Walking Past the Future, all chose certain “unconventional” jobs.

Such “unconventional” work can indeed avoid some of the burdens and monotony of ordinary labor, but it also means greater risks and requires abandoning certain moral principles, even selling one’s body and dignity. Lianrong becomes a role-playing sex worker to earn money and support her child, satisfying the various unusual sexual preferences of powerful men. Yaoting participates in drug trials to make quick money for buying a home and paying her younger sister’s tuition. Both are selling their bodies. Xiaohui becomes a waiter in a sexually oriented entertainment establishment and witnesses his girlfriend serving elderly clients. Xinmin recruits people for drug trials and accidentally pulls his long-term online girlfriend into this world. By choosing these “unconventional” jobs, they lose part of their morality and dignity, while also having to watch the people they love suffer. This is the concentrated expression of the tragedy faced by these young men and women.

When Xiaohui gives up his easy job as a waiter and returns to the hopeless Foxconn factory, he regains some spiritual dignity while at the same time making his material circumstances even worse, ultimately choosing to jump to his death. When Xinmin discovers that the girlfriend he had known online for years was in fact the girl he personally pulled into the drug-trial circle, he abandons the relatively easy money-making business of recruiting test subjects and instead goes to work at construction sites, meaning he too must face a harsher life. Between moral dignity and material gains, leaning toward one side often means losing something on the other side. For poor young people without background or connections, such painful choices are unavoidable.

Reality itself is often even more cruel than the films portray. For many migrant youths with no family or support networks in large cities, even if they wished to abandon dignity and seek morally questionable or even illegal work, such opportunities are not easily found; it is like “wanting to enter hell but finding no door.” Romance among working-class young men and women is also more realistic. This does not mean that working people lack genuine love. There is plenty of real love among them, but considerations of money and future prospects, as well as greater tendencies toward calculation and abandonment, are difficult to avoid. Their constrained living conditions and stretched incomes force them to become highly practical. Films, for dramatic purposes, often increase emotional and romantic elements while reducing the degree of utilitarian realism found in actual life.

In A Touch of Sin,  Xiaohui dies in despair, while Lianrong continues to endure humiliation and work in service jobs to support her child. In Walking Past the Future, Xinmin and Yaoting experience life’s joys and sorrows while also facing an uncertain future after Yaoting becomes seriously ill. These young lives become stained with gray far too early, already seeing the bleakness of their remaining years, some even reaching a final ending prematurely. Since China’s Reform and Opening-up (改革开放), hundreds of millions of young people have already experienced such lives, and many more of unknown numbers will likely repeat these same destinies in the future.

Although Walking Past the Future contains more brightness and hope compared to the oppressive bleakness of A Touch of Sin , its overall tone and core remain primarily tragic. While the protagonists Xinmin and Yaoting manage to survive through hardship, the death of Yaoting’s friend Li Qian (李倩) is even more dramatic and tragic. Such deaths are not purely fictional creations of film; rather, they frequently occur in reality. A girl born into poverty but possessing dreams continuously participates in drug trials to earn money for cosmetic surgery, only to die during surgery intended to make herself more beautiful. This represents a certain curse and fate of poverty. For those from poor backgrounds, pursuing lifestyles similar to those of the wealthy requires greater effort and greater risks.

Regarding the hometowns of migrant workers, A Touch of Sin presents a cruel and merciless portrayal, whereas Walking Past the Future offers a calmer and more understated depiction. The hometown in A Touch of Sin is one where the wealthy possess overwhelming power, where the poor have no path upward, and violence permeates society. This environment produces figures such as the cold-blooded killer San’er (三儿) (based on Zhou Kehua \[周克华\]), portrayed by Wang Baoqiang, and the source of murder tragedies created by Dahai (大海) (based on Hu Wenhai \[胡文海\]), portrayed by Jiang Wu. This is also why Xiaohui, unable to continue surviving in Shenzhen, would rather jump from a building than even consider returning home.

Meanwhile, Walking Past the Future provides a more direct explanation for why people would rather drift through cities such as Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, and Shenzhen than honestly return home to farm. Those who have experienced urbanization and factory work have already lost both the endurance and ability for agricultural labor and can no longer easily adapt to rural social relationships and lifestyles. The severe shortage of positions and resources in poor inland rural areas, combined with land disputes that leave people with no land to cultivate, forces them once again into wandering through large cities.

When Yaoting’s family briefly returns to their hometown in Gansu (甘肃), they discover that a sense of distance and discomfort has developed between themselves and their former home. Yaoting’s father originally came from a farming background, but after spending years working in factories and construction in large cities, he could no longer skillfully harvest corn. Young Yaoting found such agricultural work even more unbearable and soon returned to Shenzhen. Some migrant workers do not avoid returning home because they do not wish to; rather, reality itself has made rural life difficult for them to readapt to, pushing them back into drifting city lives.

The difference in tone between  A Touch of Sin and Walking Past the Future regarding workers and urban-rural depictions likely reflects not only differences in the styles and intentions of directors Jia Zhangke (贾樟柯) and Li Ruijun (李睿珺), but also the different periods in which the two films were made. A Touch of Sin was filmed in 2012, when China was energetic and rapidly developing but still relatively poor. Walking Past the Future , filmed in 2017, came after another cycle of economic growth and some improvement in people’s livelihoods. Although only five years separated them, China had already changed significantly. The differences in the mobile phones and their functions used by characters in the two films most vividly reflect these changes over only a few years. In 2012, people still primarily communicated through calls and text messages; by 2017, internet applications had become common even among ordinary migrant workers.

Yet from 2012 to 2017, material improvements in urban and rural areas did not truly change the prospects and destinies of migrant workers and the new generation of working youth. As material conditions improved, class solidification also intensified. People no longer worried about basic survival, but they remained busy and anxious. The new generation of workers hoped to buy homes in Shenzhen and other major cities throughout China and establish homes of their own. But this was far from easy. Housing prices across China were rising rapidly, outpacing income growth. Although the household registration system was gradually becoming more flexible, barriers of class and wealth still prevented migrant workers from truly settling down in cities.

Another ten years have passed, and now in 2026 housing prices have indeed fallen, but the backdrop is economic slowdown, declining incomes, increasing unemployment, and rising bankruptcies. In \\\*Walking Past the Future\\\*, Yaoting’s parents losing their jobs because of the decline of manufacturing was only a warning sign at that time; today it has become a widespread phenomenon. Yet returning to their hometowns for farming is also difficult for them. Either they search for even more exhausting jobs, or they simply consume their savings until nothing remains. Across ten years of change, young people have shifted from striving and struggling toward “lying flat” (躺平), no longer expecting hard work to elevate their social class, but instead simply drifting through life. Under such circumstances, where can the love stories of Shenzhen’s young migrant workers today still be found?

During the post-screening Q&A session for  Walking Past the Future, I asked director Li Ruijun about the differing romantic tones of the two couples in Walking Past the Future and A Touch of Sin, the changes in the mentality of Chinese youth across the decade from 2017 to 2026, and whether he planned to make new films. Director Li did not directly answer these questions. He merely said that he did not understand other directors’ thoughts, and responded with a minimalist “yes” to my question about whether he would continue making films about the lives of Chinese youth today.

Whether concerning the fate of Chinese youth more than a decade ago or today, and whether regarding the cruel reality faced by ordinary lower-class people depicted in A Touch of Sin and Walking Past the Future, all of these are rooted in China’s institutions and social structure. The reality in which family background has a greater impact on destiny than effort and hard work, the household registration system and the differences in resource allocation and social welfare attached to it, the wealth gap and class solidification, high housing prices, and increasing living costs—all of these force young men and women from poor rural families in inland China to put aside dignity and endure difficult labor merely to survive. Their chances of “turning their lives around” are extremely slim. They can only sell their labor and even their bodies like “consumable materials,” while powerful people harvest the fruits of their labor as if cutting “leeks,” enjoying the services bought with their bodies, leaving them with physical and psychological wounds. In the end comes helpless aging and silent death.

They built these beautiful cities. Whether in Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, Shenzhen, or cities throughout China, migrant workers and youth from rural backgrounds created them through labor. Without them, there would be no skylines formed by towering buildings. Even sex workers are also an indispensable part of these beautiful cities, using their bodies as a form of fuel for the nation’s prosperity since Reform and Opening-up. Yet they cannot afford the homes in the cities they themselves built. They can only live in factory dormitories or rented rooms, carefully calculating every expense while enduring difficult lives. Meanwhile, the upper classes and affluent middle classes in cities live increasingly prosperous and respectable lives.

All of this is also passed down across generations. Some people are born in Rome, while others are born as beasts of burden. Compared with older generations, the new generation of workers from poor backgrounds may seem to possess more knowledge, greater freedom, and stronger independence, yet the miserable nature of their lives continually reminds them of their class identity and their real role within cities. They cannot truly become masters of the cities they helped build, nor can they fully reintegrate into their rural hometowns, becoming people lost and without belonging both physically and spiritually.

Under material hardship and spiritual exhaustion, the love of these young working men and women is also cast under a shadow. Of course they possess love, but the burden of life forces their relationships to become simple, and such simplicity in love in turn reflects the heaviness of life. As the saying goes, “One must live before love can have something to which it may attach itself.” While loving each other, they must simultaneously confront life’s hardships and frustrations, making conflicts unavoidable and emotional breakdowns more likely. They are often forced to remain in brief moments of happiness, unable to achieve a lasting and fulfilling union. Many relationships among migrant workers end without results, and only a minority reach marriage. Those who do enter marriage face even greater challenges in the future, both personally and as families.

Walking Past the Future still romanticizes the love of workers, or perhaps uses the relatively rare cases of relationships that successfully “bear fruit” as its model. For films and television dramas, romanticized and dramatized settings are certainly more moving; if everyone remained gloomy from beginning to end, much dramatic appeal would be lost. Yet in reality, the lives of ordinary poor people are indeed more depressing and monotonous, and love rarely contains so much romance and emotional entanglement. This is not because poor people are unworthy of romantic love, but because reality forces them into pessimism and practicality, making lighthearted happiness difficult. Furthermore, choosing not to abandon a seriously ill lover and instead entering marriage is an even rarer decision.

Today’s Chinese youth from poor rural families, and more broadly young people from ordinary Chinese families, face a new era and environment different from those of their grandparents and parents, yet they also face similar disadvantages and lack of opportunities arising from social stratification. They remain troubled and occupied by concerns over food, clothing, housing, and transportation. These young lives move from innocence to maturity in confusion, gradually losing vitality while their minds become burdened. Very few manage to “defy fate and rewrite destiny”; most can only experience fast-food-style lives and fast-food-style love. If family crises or illness strike them, they can only helplessly accept unfortunate destinies, abandoning early the dream of struggling for a secure life and drifting through the remainder of their existence in confusion.

Fairly speaking, Walking Past the Future is not an exceptionally remarkable film. Compared with works such as  A Touch of Sin, it is much more subdued, and its artistic quality is not particularly outstanding. Yet it still presents the struggles and confusion, lives and destinies of young people from poor Chinese families, and the love shared by young men and women who retain sincere emotions amid such hardships. Such documentation and portrayal, giving these people a voice and allowing China and the world to see them, is itself valuable. Director Li Ruijun comes from Gansu, and since the film uses a family from Gansu as its background, his speaking for the people of his hometown deserves special praise. As someone from Henan (河南), I likewise hope for more excellent films about the local customs, culture, and history of Henan. China needs more voices and images that reflect social realities, tell the stories of ordinary people, and speak on behalf of those on the margins and the disadvantaged.

(This article was written by Wang Qingmin (王庆民), a Chinese writer living in Europe.)

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. Two audiobooks, one Brian De Palma: THE DEVIL'S CANDY: THE ANATOMY OF A HOLLYWOOD FIASCO (1991) and THE WORLD IS YOURS:THE WORLD IS YOURS: THE STORY OF SCARFACE (2024)

**Score:** 8
**Body:** I recently enjoyed the audiobook versions of two books centered on De Palma, one of my favorite filmmakers: Glenn Kenny's THE WORLD IS YOURS: THE STORY OF SCARFACE (2024) and Julie Salamon's THE DEVIL'S CANDY: THE ANATOMY OF A HOLLYWOOD FIASCO (1991). The latter is a behind-the-scenes account of the making of THE BONFIRE OF THE VANITIES (1990), which might have marked the low point of De Palma's career.

If you also happen to be a fan of the oft-controversial director, I recommend checking out these two books. And if, like me, you rarely have much free time on your hands, I recommend going for the audiobooks since you can listen to them while doing something else.

Having listened to THE WORLD IS YOURS and THE DEVIL'S CANDY back to back, what I found especially interesting (besides the extensive level of detail revealing exactly how much goes into making a big studio picture, even one that ends up being deemed one of the worst ever) is the not-so-simple portrait they paint of De Palma across a very fateful decade.

In both books he comes across as a master of the technical aspects of his craft, his faith in his artistic vision being unshakable at times, for better or worse. However, by the time he reached THE BONFIRE OF THE VANITIES, De Palma had experienced a pair of high-profile flops (albeit bookended around his 1987 hit adaptation of THE UNTOUCHABLES), and as such, seems more susceptible to the pressures placed upon him.

Now of course that's a simplification and the two productions were different in some very key ways. I also don't want to give away too much lest anybody wants to track down THE WORLD IS YOURS and THE DEVIL'S CANDY and get the full story for themselves.

The point is these two audiobooks get my full recommendation and so I wanted to mention them to other cinephiles. If you have recommendations for similar behind-the-scenes books about notable film productions, please share them, especially if I can listen to them while doing other things.

## 4. Why are we so obsessed with ratings and awards? And why do we often disapprove of some of them? Some thoughts

**Score:** 0
**Body:** (The title sounds absolute, I know some people don't care.)

I believe the main reason is that people desire consensus, validation, and a sense of certainty. Many of us want others to mirror what we feel and to recognize the things we love. Obsessing over how a film makes it to “the canon” or a favorite director winning an award can become part of how we perform identity and how we feel a sense of belonging.

However, judging art objectively is nearly impossible because there is no ultimate, universal standard for evaluation, as it often depends on the combination of relevant history, social context, and, sometimes, prevailing intellectual trends. We try to grasp and construct an order in a sea of subjectivity. What awards and ratings can do is stabilize opinions and shape consensus to guide people and define a shared standard.

Given people's limited attention and time, awards, ratings, and rankings play key roles in spotlighting artists and guiding audiences. For audiences, these accolades are more digestible and more efficient at signaling the value of a work than articulating feelings and thoughts with reasons. For artists, receiving an award can enhance their power and influence, and possibly lead to greater creative freedom in future projects. Despite many people saying the Oscars and Grammys don't matter every year, these awards can still empower artists within the larger discourse.

Consensus can also serve as an ideological signal, shaping the ecosystem of our film culture. What stories and themes get uplifted? What aesthetics and styles are preferred? Sometimes, it seems the canon is less about the so-called “inherent merit” and more a mirror of dominant values. Aligning with dominant values gives people power and confidence.

This could be part of the reason why some of the most common posts you see on Film Reddit or Film Twitter are those in which people say certain acclaimed or popular films and directors are overrated. Because by pushing back, people are “fighting” for their values and what those values represent. Liking a film or other media, especially when discussing it online, is not just about personal taste but also a signal of curated identity, because a work's reception reflects as much on viewers as on authors. When we see people display their rankings or the Oscars results, we tend to judge others’ tastes more than the works themselves.

And consensus (or what culture is dominant) is never stable, especially nowadays, because feedback from different communities, environments, and discourses influences how people perceive the work and, then, the consensus itself. Look at EEAAO, Anora, and even Emilia Perez. They were acclaimed when they premiered at film festivals, but their reception (especially the latter) shifted drastically once various online communities got involved. I think in the future, it will be harder for a film to become a stable part of the “important cinema” canon or a high status work like The Godfather, because we are in a different cultural ecosystem and also under the influence of fragmental algorithms. Parasite is the only more recent film I can think of now, but even it faced some socioeconomic criticisms in its home country.

There is a risk that scoring and awarding become a social performance, with people caring more about how others view the work and us than about our personal viewing experience. It happened to me before. I think, to some extent, Susan Sontag’s Against Interpretation applies here, that awards can potentially reduce experience to mere meaning, and stars become convenient replacements for the direct emotional engagement.

I disagree with everything you have written, but this part I think is wrong in an interesting way. In truth, consensus is more stable than ever and that's a huge problem for film. 

(What you're describing with, like, Emilia Perez was a consensus forming, not switch btw)

In the 20th century, it's not that the consensus would change because more people would come in and outnumber the people who like the movie, it's the case that people changed their minds about movies. And we don't anymore because we're on the record in a way we never were before. 

The truth is if you took a poll of general audiences in 1980, they really did prefer Ordinary People to Raging Bull. Not universally, of course, but if Best Picture had been a referendum, RB still would have lost.

But people changed their mind, *certainly without having to have been publicly wrong and often without even knowing*. Because while they had opinions they hadn't publicly signed their name to it. 

Today, we don't just have our opinions, we publish them (on letterboxd or social media or in the group chat or whatever). This causes calcification. No one wants to go back and apologize for Reddit posts arguing Oppenheimer is the strongest movie in Barbenheimer, especially since people wouldn't come to that change of heart at the same time, so the chances of that consensus shift happening is lower than it would have been in the 20th century (which is unfortunate cause Barbie is the better movie in Barbenheimer).
